How to Draw a Flow Chart From Scratch (Symbols + Tools + Worked Example)

A flow chart is the picture version of an SOP — a visual way to show how a process runs. The standard symbols are Terminal (start/end), Process (step), Decision (branching condition), and Arrowhead (direction). The most popular free tool is Draw.io.
Flow charts are really useful when you need to untangle how a feature actually works. So this post walks through how to draw one, and the drawing tools I've used over the years.
A flow chart's use cases are pretty broad — feature design, analysis, mapping out a user flow, all kinds of things. If that's still abstract, the simplest framing is: a flow chart is the
Why bother learning to draw them? As a software engineer, you'll often need to clarify how a feature works. You

describe it in text, but text gets ambiguous — the same word can mean different things to different people, and you end up arguing about definitions instead of the actual logic. Pictures cut through that, which is why a lot of design docs include flow charts.
I drew quite a few flow charts back in college for school projects. There are more conventions to it than you'd expect.
There's no rule that says you have to use specific software — even drawing on paper works. But here are a few I've used:
Diagram Designer was what I used in college — it's Windows-only. For this post I'll use
Before drawing, you need to know the standard symbols. Each one has a specific meaning, and using the wrong one will make your chart hard to read.
The arrowhead is just an arrow connected by a line. It tells the reader the direction of flow — from A to B, or B to C.
A rounded rectangle, used to mark the start and end of a flow.
"Decision" can sound abstract — think of it as a "judgment" instead. If condition A is met, the flow goes to B; otherwise it goes to C. In code terms, it's basically
This one represents where data comes from or goes to — typing in an email/password, for example.
The Process symbol can feel less obvious. For a web app, you can think of it as a page — login page, registration page, that kind of thing.
That covers the most common symbols. There are more, but these are enough to draw most charts.
Drawing a chart out of thin air is hard, so let me work through an example: a user registration flow.
We start with a "Terminal" symbol marking the beginning:
Then an "Arrowhead" leading to a "Process" symbol. Why Process? Because the user lands on the registration page first:
Next, an "Input/Output" symbol — the user enters their email and password:
Then comes the key moment. After they submit, we need to check whether the email is already registered. That's a "Decision":

Decision symbols always branch into multiple lines — in this example, one for "email already registered", one for "email available". Arrowheads carry the flow forward, and the lines are usually labelled with the condition:

Let's follow the "registration failed" path first. After failure, the page should show "Email already registered" — then loop back to "show registration page" so the user can try again. A complete flow chart always has a start and an end, with no dead ends, so we wire that loop back:

Now the "success" path. After registration succeeds, the page shows "Registration successful" — but is that the end? Probably not. Depending on your product, you might redirect them to the login page or the home page. Let's say we route them to "show home page":
Finally, add a "Terminal" symbol to mark the end:
And that's a complete registration flow chart. Here's the full version:
A few key takeaways about flow charts:
Don't misuse symbols — each one has a specific meaning, use the right one
Every symbol should have a text label, otherwise readers can't follow
Every chart needs a clear start and end — no dead ends in the middle
Also, flow charts don't have to be drawn vertically. Horizontal layouts are common too. This post focuses on vertical, but the principles are the same.

Flow charts are great for untangling how a feature actually works. This post walks through the basic symbols, my favourite drawing tools, and a real example: charting out a user registration flow from start to finish.
